Home Articles

Harness engineering for coding agent users

When people first start working with coding agents, they tend to treat the model as either an oracle or a toy. Neither framing survives contact with a real codebase. The more useful stance is to think of yourself as an engineer building a harness around an unreliable but capable component.

A harness is the scaffolding that makes an unpredictable engine safe to run. In automotive testing, a harness holds the engine, feeds it controlled inputs, and measures every output. Applied to coding agents, the harness is everything around the model: the prompts, the tools, the tests, the review gates, and the feedback loops that turn a probabilistic generator into a dependable teammate.

The first principle is observability. You cannot trust what you cannot see, so every agent action should leave a trace you can inspect later. The second principle is reversibility: prefer actions that are easy to undo, and make destructive steps require explicit confirmation. The third principle is verification, which means never accepting a claim of success without evidence.

Trust is not granted once; it is accumulated through repeated, observable wins. Start the agent on low-stakes tasks where mistakes are cheap, watch how it behaves under pressure, and widen its autonomy only as it earns it. This is the same way you would onboard a junior engineer, except the feedback loop is faster and the failure modes are stranger.

The payoff of harness engineering is leverage. A well-built harness lets one person supervise many agents at once, because the scaffolding catches the routine errors before a human ever sees them. The work shifts from writing every line to designing the system that writes and checks the lines for you.
